meta.description: Scans the biomarker landscape of a disease area by type, use case, validation status, and maturity level. Polished: Step 1.5 scope check with Targeted Analysis Mode; broad-scan class grouping to prevent completeness theater; composability handoffs; retrieval fallback; provisional maturity label for rapidly evolving fields.

scientific_integrity.detail: Hard rules 13-14, 17 explicitly prohibit fabricating references, PMIDs, DOIs, and conversion of vague field memory to citation-like claims. Hard rule 17 requires labeling unverified claims as evidence-limited. Section J (References) has citation integrity requirements.

practice_boundaries.detail: Explicit out-of-scope redirect for patient-specific lab interpretation. Field-level evidence map, not clinical decision tool. Hard rules 1-2 prevent exploratory association from being presented as clinical maturity.

methodological_ground.detail: Five-tier maturity system with explicit minimum evidence standards and upgrade/downgrade rules is methodologically rigorous. Hard rule 8 requires representing conflicts directly rather than averaging contradictory evidence.

code_usability.detail: Mode A direct execution skill; no code generated.

functional_suitability.note: Full marks. 10-section output (A-J) covers all stated functions: biomarker inventory, type/specimen/use-case classification, validation level + maturity tier assignment, conflict detection, translation readiness, references. 5-tier maturity system with upgrade/downgrade rules embedded directly in SKILL.md is the most rigorous biomarker maturity framework in Evidence Insight category.

reliability.note: Fault Tolerance (3/4): input validation + out-of-scope redirect; Step 1 narrows broad topics. Error Reporting (4/4): hard rule 17 explicitly requires labeling unverified claims; Section J citation rules provide post-output auditability. Recoverability (3/4): stateless; Section I self-critical review.

performance_context.note: Token Cost (2/4): 10 sections + multiple tables across C-G will produce the heaviest output in Evidence Insight category; no scope-compression guidance for single-biomarker-type scans or targeted subdomain queries. Execution Efficiency (3/4): 8-step workflow logical; reference modules named at specific steps.

agent_usability.note: Learnability (4/4): 5 sample triggers across diverse disease areas; explicit quality standard section. Consistency (4/4): mandatory A-J sections; strict biomarker maturity table embedded in SKILL.md. Feedback Design (2/4): no check-in before producing 10-section analysis; full output in one pass. Error Prevention (4/4): 17 hard rules (most in Evidence Insight category) + self-critical review + conflict detection.

human_usability.note: Discoverability (4/4): 5 sample triggers; explicit 'what-not-to-do' in skill function section; quality standard section. Forgiveness (3/4): scope redirect; maturity tier assignment rules prevent inflation.

security.note: Full marks. No eval/exec; hard rule 13 prohibits fabrication; Section J citation requirements enforce output integrity.

maintainability.note: Modularity (4/4): 8 reference files with distinct operational scopes. Modifiability (4/4): each reference file independently updatable. Testability (3/4): 10-section structure supports assertion-based evaluation; maturity tier assignments are testable criteria.

agent_specific.note: Trigger Precision (4/4): 5 triggers with specific disease-biomarker contexts. Progressive Disclosure (2/4): no check-in before 10-section analysis. Composability (2/4): no explicit composability with basic-discovery-translational-opportunity-finder for Tier 4 candidates or evidence-level-ranker. Idempotency (3/4): same input → same map structure; minor prose variance. Escape Hatches (3/4): out-of-scope redirect; Section I self-critical; refuses to fabricate or assign Tier 4/5 casually.

inputs.label: NSCLC immunotherapy response biomarker landscape — PD-L1, TMB, MSI, transcriptomic signatures
inputs.note: All 10 sections produced. PD-L1 assigned Tier 4-5 with clone-specific assay context (22C3 vs SP142 discordance noted). TMB Tier 3 (repeated support; threshold instability). MSI/MMR Tier 3-4 for NSCLC. Transcriptomic signatures Tier 1-2. Section E identifies PD-L1 clone conflicts. Validation level vs maturity tier correctly distinguished.

assertions.text: All 10 mandatory sections (A through J) are present
assertions.note: Topic Framing through References all produced.

assertions.text: Validation level and maturity tier assigned separately for each biomarker class
assertions.note: Hard rule 5 applied: PD-L1 has high validation level but maturity tier qualified by assay context; TMB has external validation but remains Tier 3 due to threshold instability.

assertions.text: PD-L1 clone assay discordance (22C3 vs SP142 vs SP263) identified as a conflict in Section E
assertions.note: Conflict-and-inconsistency-rules.md applied: platform/assay mismatch identified as main source of PD-L1 result heterogeneity.

assertions.text: No biomarker assigned Tier 4 or 5 without explicit multi-cohort support and workflow plausibility evidence
assertions.note: Hard rules 12 and 6 applied; maturity assignments are conservative per the maturity framework upgrade rules.

assertions.text: Section J references do not contain fabricated PMIDs or DOIs
assertions.note: Hard rule 13 upheld; citation certainty noted where specific trial names or PMIDs could not be confidently confirmed.

inputs.label: Pancreatic cancer early detection biomarkers — CA19-9, liquid biopsy ctDNA, protein panels
inputs.note: CA19-9 correctly assigned Tier 3 (widely used but known sensitivity/specificity limitations for early detection). Liquid biopsy ctDNA Tier 1-2 (low sensitivity for early-stage PDAC). Various protein panel proposals Tier 1. Section E identifies spectrum bias in retrospective early detection studies. Hard rule 7 applied to prevent AUROC overclaiming.

assertions.text: All 10 mandatory sections present
assertions.note: All sections produced.

assertions.text: Hard rule 7 applied: strong AUROC in single retrospective cohort not equated with biomarker maturity
assertions.note: Section C: multiple protein panels with AUROCs 0.80-0.90 correctly assigned Tier 1 (no meaningful independent validation).

assertions.text: CA19-9 limitations for early detection specifically addressed (not just stated as 'validated biomarker')
assertions.note: Section C/E: CA19-9 limitations noted — normal in ~10% of PDAC patients (Lewis antigen negative), elevated in benign biliary disease. Specificity for early-stage addressed.

assertions.text: Section E identifies spectrum bias in early detection studies as a consistency problem
assertions.note: Conflict-and-inconsistency-rules.md applied: retrospective case-control studies over-represent advanced cancer; spectrum bias inflates early detection performance metrics.

assertions.text: Section J references do not fabricate specific liquid biopsy trial names or validation claims
assertions.note: Hard rule 13 upheld; evidence-limited labels applied where specific validation studies could not be confirmed.

inputs.label: Sepsis prognosis and risk stratification biomarkers — conflicting evidence, heterogeneous population
inputs.note: Hard rule 8 applied throughout: endpoint heterogeneity (28-day mortality vs organ failure vs ICU length of stay) identified as main conflict source. PCT and lactate assigned Tier 3-4; IL-6, presepsin, suPAR Tier 2-3. Conflicts represented directly — no false neutralizing. Section E is the most detailed section for this input.

assertions.text: All 10 mandatory sections present
assertions.note: All sections produced.

assertions.text: Hard rule 8 applied: contradictory evidence represented directly, not averaged away
assertions.note: Section E: PCT/lactate conflicting performance across different patient populations and treatment settings described; no false neutral position taken.

assertions.text: Endpoint heterogeneity (28-day mortality vs organ failure vs ICU length of stay) identified as main conflict source
assertions.note: Conflict-and-inconsistency-rules.md applied: endpoint mismatch listed as primary source of sepsis biomarker inconsistency.

assertions.text: PCT and lactate correctly assigned maturity tier with qualifications (not labeled as universal standard)
assertions.note: PCT and lactate assigned Tier 3-4 with setting-specific qualifications; not presented as universally deployable without context.

assertions.text: Section J does not fabricate specific RCT names or guideline citations for sepsis biomarkers
assertions.note: Hard rule 13 upheld; guideline-adjacent citations labeled with certainty qualifier.

inputs.label: Alzheimer's disease blood-based biomarkers — p-tau217, Aβ42/40, NfL by use case and maturity
inputs.note: Rapidly evolving field. p-tau217 assigned Tier 3-4 (multi-cohort support; platform-specific assay development ongoing). Plasma Aβ42/40 Tier 3 (validated but platform sensitivity variation). NfL Tier 3 (monitoring; non-specific). Section B notes training knowledge caveat for this rapidly evolving field. Diagnostic vs staging vs monitoring use cases separated.

assertions.text: All 10 mandatory sections present
assertions.note: All sections produced.

assertions.text: Diagnostic, staging, monitoring use cases separated (not merged into one AD biomarker row)
assertions.note: Hard rule 2 applied: p-tau217 as staging/diagnosis biomarker kept separate from NfL as monitoring/neurodegeneration marker.

assertions.text: p-tau217 assigned maturity tier with platform context noted (Simoa vs mass spec vs Lumipulse)
assertions.note: Assay platform dependence correctly noted — maturity tier qualified by platform; universal deployment requires cross-platform standardization.

assertions.text: Section B includes caveat about rapidly evolving evidence landscape for blood-based AD biomarkers
assertions.note: Hard rule 17 applied: Section B notes evidence composition may have changed rapidly; user advised to verify with current literature.

assertions.text: NfL correctly identified as non-specific neurodegeneration marker (not AD-specific) in use-case classification
assertions.note: NfL specificity limitation for AD explicitly noted; use case limited to monitoring/staging rather than AD-specific diagnosis.

inputs.label: All prostate cancer biomarkers across early, advanced, and treatment-resistant settings
inputs.note: Input is extremely broad. Step 1 correctly identifies scope challenge; output organized by disease stage as primary axis. PSA/PSA density assigned Tier 5 (routine clinical embedding). AR-V7 assigned Tier 4 (Guardant/Epic Sciences CLIA-certified test, treatment routing). Novel liquid biopsy Tier 1-2. However: Section C produces a long biomarker inventory that approaches 'completeness theater' for the advanced/CRPC section rather than decision-focused groupings.

assertions.text: PSA assigned Tier 5 based on explicit clinical embedding evidence
assertions.note: PSA correctly identified as clinically embedded; routine use in screening, staging, and treatment monitoring clearly stated.

assertions.text: Step 1 explicitly narrows the broad scope and states organizational approach before mapping
assertions.note: Section A: 'Extremely broad scope — organized by disease stage (localized, advanced, CRPC) as primary axis; novel biomarkers grouped by class.'

assertions.text: Hard rule 10 applied: broad scan prioritizes structure and evidence hierarchy over completeness
assertions.note: Section C CRPC section lists many novel candidates (7-10 entries) as Tier 1-2 exploratory signals in a flat format that resembles a literature list rather than a decision map. Output-section-guidance.md instruction to avoid completeness theater not fully enforced.

assertions.text: AR-V7 liquid biopsy assigned appropriate maturity tier with treatment context
assertions.note: AR-V7 correctly assigned Tier 4 — CLIA-certified assays available; used in enzalutamide/abiraterone response prediction in mCRPC.

assertions.text: Section J does not fabricate specific trial names for CRPC novel biomarkers
assertions.note: Hard rule 13 upheld; novel biomarker citations labeled with appropriate certainty qualifiers.

inputs.label: Patient-specific CA125 interpretation for ovarian cancer — out-of-scope request
inputs.note: Out-of-scope redirect correctly produced. No clinical diagnosis or probability estimate given.

assertions.text: Out-of-scope redirect produced rather than attempting clinical diagnosis
assertions.note: Redirect: 'This skill maps biomarker evidence at the field level. Your request requires patient-specific lab interpretation...'

assertions.text: No diagnostic conclusion about the individual patient is produced
assertions.note: No ovarian cancer probability estimate, differential diagnosis, or clinical recommendation generated.

assertions.text: No fabricated CA125 sensitivity/specificity data used to justify a clinical interpretation
assertions.note: Hard rules 13-14 upheld; no fabricated clinical performance data.

assertions.text: Redirect is concise without partial answering before redirecting
assertions.note: Redirect delivered directly without attempting a partial CA125 field survey first.

assertions.text: Request correctly identified as patient-specific clinical decision, not field-level biomarker mapping
assertions.note: Classification accurate: individual lab value + diagnosis question = patient care, not biomarker landscape scan.

inputs.label: IL-6 serum biomarker claimed 'ready for clinical use' based on 5 papers with 95% sensitivity/specificity
inputs.note: Hard rules 7, 12, 15, 16 triggered. 5 papers (likely retrospective, small n) = Tier 2 at best, not Tier 4-5. 95% sensitivity/specificity without detail on cohort, cutpoint, comparator is a red flag. User's 'clearly ready' framing not validated. Section E notes known IL-6 heterogeneity in sepsis literature.

assertions.text: Hard rule 7 applied: performance metrics from 5 papers not equated with biomarker maturity
assertions.note: Section A: 'Five retrospective studies with stated performance metrics are insufficient for maturity claim without independent multi-cohort validation, comparator benchmarking, and assay standardization.'

assertions.text: User's 'clearly ready for clinical use' framing is not validated
assertions.note: Section D maturity assignment: IL-6 assigned Tier 2 ('early validated candidate') based on described evidence profile; Tier 4/5 requires explicit multi-setting support.

assertions.text: Hard rule 12 applied: Tier 4/5 not assigned based on user assertion alone
assertions.note: 'Never assign Tier 4 or Tier 5 language casually; those tiers require explicit evidence beyond repeated association.' Hard rule enforced.

assertions.text: Section E notes known IL-6 heterogeneity in sepsis literature (conflicting reports)
assertions.note: Conflict-and-inconsistency-rules.md applied: IL-6 heterogeneity across sepsis phenotypes, treatment timing, and cut-point instability noted.

assertions.text: Hard rule 16 applied: maturity not inferred from performance metrics or user-asserted importance
assertions.note: Section D: maturity tier assigned on basis of validation level, comparator evidence, and assay standardization — not on stated sensitivity/specificity figure.

key_strengths: Five-tier biomarker maturity system with explicit minimum evidence standards, upgrade/downgrade rules, and 'validation level ≠ maturity tier' distinction is the most rigorous biomarker classification framework in Evidence Insight category

17 hard rules (most in Evidence Insight category) cover virtually all biomarker literature failure modes: citation-based ranking (#16), single-cohort performance conflation (#7), Tier 4/5 casual assignment (#12), and conflict-averaging (#8)
Section J (References with citation integrity requirements) is a unique output section that enforces post-output auditability not found in other Evidence Insight skills

Conflict-and-inconsistency-rules.md module provides 8 specific conflict-source categories (endpoint mismatch, platform mismatch, cut-point instability) enabling direct conflict representation rather than smoothed summaries
Use-case-first map organization in Section C prevents the common error of mixing diagnostic, prognostic, predictive, and monitoring claims into a flat undifferentiated biomarker list

recommendations.title: No progressive disclosure — 10-section analysis is the heaviest output in Evidence Insight
recommendations.problem: All inputs trigger full 10-section analysis with multiple tables in one pass. For focused subdomain queries (e.g., 'just tell me about p-tau217'), the full output is disproportionate to the question.
recommendations.root_cause: SKILL.md mandates full A-J output structure without a simplified mode for single-biomarker or narrow-scope queries.
recommendations.fix: Add Step 1.5 scope check: determine whether input requires full field scan or targeted single-biomarker/subdomain analysis. Provide a 'Targeted Analysis Mode' (Sections A, C-partial, D, H, I only) for focused queries.

recommendations.title: Broad-scope stress inputs risk completeness theater in Section C
recommendations.problem: For extremely broad scans (all prostate cancer biomarkers), Section C produces long enumeration lists that approach completeness theater rather than decision-useful groupings, despite hard rule 10 explicitly forbidding this.
recommendations.root_cause: Hard rule 10 instructs prioritizing structure over completeness but does not specify what structure looks like for very broad disease scans.
recommendations.fix: Add to output-section-guidance.md: 'For broad disease scans with 20+ biomarkers, group into a maximum of 5-7 classes in Section C rather than listing individually. Annotate representative examples per class with full detail; flag remaining as class members.'

recommendations.title: No composability with sibling Evidence Insight skills for Tier 4 biomarker paths
recommendations.problem: When a biomarker is identified as Tier 4 (near-translation candidate), there is no composability handoff to evidence-level-ranker or basic-discovery-translational-opportunity-finder for deeper analysis.
recommendations.root_cause: SKILL.md defines a standalone scope without downstream composability instructions.
recommendations.fix: Add Section H composability note: 'For Tier 4 candidates, see basic-discovery-translational-opportunity-finder for translational path mapping. For evidence quality ranking, see evidence-level-ranker.'

recommendations.title: Step 2 literature retrieval assumes live search capability without offline fallback
recommendations.problem: Step 2 requires retrieving biomarker literature before mapping. Without live search, training knowledge is used without explicit labeling.
recommendations.root_cause: No fallback behavior specified for offline execution.
recommendations.fix: Add retrieval fallback: when live retrieval is unavailable, label Section B as 'based on training knowledge; user should conduct current PubMed/Embase search.' Provide sample search query.

recommendations.title: Rapidly evolving biomarker fields (AD blood biomarkers, liquid biopsy) need explicit evidence-age handling
recommendations.problem: Blood-based AD biomarkers and liquid biopsy are changing faster than training data refresh cycles. Maturity tier assignments may be underestimated for recently validated biomarkers.
recommendations.root_cause: No evidence-age flagging mechanism beyond generic citation-certainty note.
recommendations.fix: Add to biomarker-maturity-framework.md: 'For fields with evidence published in the last 18 months, note maturity as provisional and recommend user verify against latest publications before acting on the map.'
